one_line_takeaway: By encoding the entire multibit payload at every token position via binomial (rather than positional) encoding, and adding a stateful bit-prioritization mechanism, this scheme beats 8 prior multibit LLM watermarks on message/bit accuracy and scaling with text length, but the paper's own robustness experiments show all such watermarks, including theirs, still collapse to near-random accuracy under paraphrasing.
key_finding: Across message accuracy vs. quality trade-off curves (Fig. 2), the stateful variant Ours+ outperforms all 8 baselines for the larger, more practical payloads (32 and 64 bits), with the gap widening at longer payloads and in low-distortion (low-perplexity) regimes; the stateless variant Ours achieves higher BA@1%FPR (Fig. 4) than baselines but lower message accuracy than Ours+, confirming an explicit trade-off the authors identify between the two proposed encoders. On scaling with generation length (Fig. 3, 32-bit payload, low-distortion regime), their scheme scales more favorably with token count than all baselines; at 500 tokens their bit accuracy exceeds 90% while the best baseline reaches only around 80% (Fig. 9/D.2). For robustness (Table 1, 32-bit bitstring), Ours+ has the highest bit accuracy in nearly every row: e.g., at 10% word deletion 0.707 vs. next-best BiMark/MC2Mark at 0.664, and at 10% synonym substitution 0.707 vs. 0.671 (MC2Mark); but all schemes, including Ours+, collapse toward the random baseline of 0.5 under paraphrasing (Ours+ ends at 0.509) and message accuracy for every scheme is already effectively null at just 10% word/synonym edits. The paper also demonstrates that two established baselines, MPAC and StealthInk, have miscalibrated zero-bit p-values (empirical FPR above the nominal/theoretical FPR on 1000 human-written C4 texts) before the authors' proposed Monte-Carlo recalibration is applied.
core_contribution: The paper introduces a new multibit LLM watermarking scheme based on 'binomial encoding': instead of allocating each generation step to encode a single bit of an m-bit payload (the dominant prior-art strategy, called position allocation), the method encodes the FULL m-bit bitstring at EVERY token position. At each step it draws m independent Bernoulli(0.5) pseudorandom score vectors (one per payload bit), complements each according to the target bit value (Eq. 2), sums them into a binomial score vector, and biases the next-token distribution toward tokens whose score aligns with the whole message. Decoding takes the same PRNG-derived per-token Bernoulli values and does a majority vote per bit across the sequence (Eq. 4), with a two-sided binomial test giving a per-bit p-value/confidence for free. On top of this stateless base scheme, the authors add an optional 'stateful encoder' (Sec. 3.2) that, at each generation step, computes each bit's expected worst-case future decoding accuracy in closed form (Lemma 3.1, a normal-CDF approximation) and reweights encoding pressure toward currently under-encoded bits, trading some statistical confidence for higher message accuracy. The full pipeline (Sec. 3.3) instantiates this with the 'Soft PPL' zero-bit watermarking scheme from a companion paper (Gloaguen et al., a unified LLM watermark framework) via an argmax-based logit modification (Eq. 8), with an alternative SynthID-based instantiation given in an appendix. A second, separate contribution (Sec. 4) is methodological/critical: the paper argues that essentially all prior multibit watermark evaluations (Yoo et al. MPAC, Qu et al. RSBH, Jiang et al. MirrorMark, etc.) report bit/message accuracy only on text already known to be watermarked, silently assuming away the detection question of whether a message is present at all; the paper proposes a new metric, bit accuracy at alpha% FPR (BA@alpha%FPR), that folds in the false-positive/no-message case, analogous to TPR@FPR for zero-bit watermarks.
threat_model: This is a content-provenance/traceability mechanism, not an attacker-defender security paper in the adversarial-ML sense; there is no adversary trying to fool a classifier via crafted inputs, and no discussion of prompt injection, jailbreaks, or model compromise. The relevant 'threat' framing is: (1) a downstream consumer of arbitrary text (which may or may not have been produced by the watermarked LLM) must decide whether it is watermarked and, if so, recover an embedded payload (e.g., user ID, timestamp, licensing metadata) without access to the LLM itself, while controlling the false-positive rate of wrongly detecting a message in non-watermarked (human-authored) text; (2) an adversary or ordinary usage may apply text modifications (word deletion, synonym substitution, paraphrasing) that degrade or destroy the embedded signal, and the paper evaluates robustness against these under Sec. 5.3, finding the watermark's bit accuracy degrades toward the random baseline of 0.5 under paraphrasing for every scheme tested, including their own. No adaptive/white-box adversary against the watermark key or PRNG is modeled; robustness is only tested against generic text-editing perturbations, not targeted watermark-removal attacks.
limitations: The authors explicitly state (Sec 6, Limitations, verbatim in substance): robustness of multibit LLM watermarks 'is still an ongoing challenge' -- while their approach outperforms all evaluated baselines, it remains limited in practical scenarios, particularly under paraphrasing, where bit accuracy for every tested scheme (including theirs) becomes almost random and message accuracy is essentially null after even mild (10%) text edits. They also state they provide no theoretical proof that non-position-allocation methods are strictly better than position-allocation-based methods, only empirical evidence, leaving this as an open direction. Additional caveats surfaced in the paper body: several baselines had to be reimplemented from scratch (ArcMark, MC2Mark, MirrorMark) absent official code, which could introduce fidelity gaps versus the original authors' implementations; ArcMark and Cycle-Shift decoding time scales exponentially with payload length, limiting their applicability to the longer (32/64-bit) payloads emphasized as most practically relevant; RSBH has no published configuration for 64-bit payloads and is excluded there; the stateful encoder's benefit is coupled to an explicit, acknowledged trade-off (it improves message accuracy but reduces per-bit statistical confidence/BA@alpha%FPR, and underperforms the stateless variant on zero-bit TPR@1%FPR, Sec D.1) rather than being a strict improvement on all axes.

datasets_used: ELI5 (long-form QA prompts, Fan et al. 2019)
C4 realnewslike subset (for zero-bit FPR calibration on human text and for RSBH frequency-balanced mapping)
quantitative_results: Robustness Table 1, 10% word deletion, bit accuracy: Ours+ 0.707 vs BiMark 0.664, MC2Mark 0.664, MPAC 0.609, StealthInk 0.606, MirrorMark 0.539, RSBH 0.511
Robustness Table 1, 10% synonym substitution, bit accuracy: Ours+ 0.707 vs MC2Mark 0.671, BiMark 0.666, StealthInk 0.609, MPAC 0.612, MirrorMark 0.544, RSBH 0.509
Robustness Table 1, paraphrasing, bit accuracy: Ours+ 0.509 (near-random 0.5), best among baselines being MC2Mark 0.509/BiMark 0.504, i.e., all schemes collapse toward 0.5
Scaling with tokens (32-bit, low-distortion regime, Fig 9/App D.2): at 500 tokens Ours/Ours+ bit accuracy exceeds 90% vs best baseline ~80%
Message accuracy after 10% word/synonym edits: 'already null' for all tested schemes

Zero-bit p-value miscalibration: MPAC and StealthInk empirical FPR exceeds theoretical/expected FPR on 1000 human-authored 200-token C4 realnewslike texts before Monte-Carlo recalibration (Fig 6 left) vs. calibrated afterward (Fig 6 right)
